topic: Area of Study 3: Music for Stage and Screen
subject: music

items.q: Is all music in a film diegetic?
items.correct: No - only music whose source exists in the story world, like a car radio, is diegetic; orchestral underscore is non-diegetic.
items.misconception: Diegetic just means any music used in a film.
items.random_wrong: Diegetic music is music recorded in a digital studio.
items.why: The terms classify where the music sits relative to the story world; using 'diegetic' for everything makes scene analysis impossible.

items.q: Must a leitmotif sound exactly the same every time it returns?
items.correct: No - composers change its key, orchestration, tempo and mood so the theme tracks the character's journey.
items.misconception: A leitmotif has to be repeated identically or it stops being a leitmotif.
items.random_wrong: A leitmotif may only be heard during battle scenes.
items.why: Transformation is the technique's expressive power: the same melodic identity in a new guise tells the audience how circumstances have changed.

items.q: Is word painting only found in old choral music?
items.correct: No - musical theatre uses it constantly, for example rising lines and soaring phrases setting words about flying in 'Defying Gravity'.
items.misconception: Word painting died out with Renaissance madrigals.
items.random_wrong: Word painting means projecting the lyrics onto the scenery.
items.why: Matching music to lyric meaning is a timeless songwriting craft; identifying it in modern show tunes earns analysis marks in this area of study.

items.q: Who composed the Star Wars Main Title music?
items.why: Correct answer: John Williams. “Hans Zimmer” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 'Defying Gravity' comes from which stage musical?
items.why: It's Wicked. “Cats” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A leitmotif is best described as what?
items.correct: A recurring theme linked to a character or idea
items.misconception: A drum pattern played in every scene
items.why: Correct answer: A recurring theme linked to a character or idea. “A drum pattern played in every scene” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Non-diegetic film music is music that is heard by whom?
items.correct: Only the audience, not the characters
items.misconception: Only the characters in the story
items.why: It's Only the audience, not the characters. “Only the characters in the story” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was the first Star Wars film, with John Williams's famous score, released?
items.why: The real figure is 1977. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
